response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental mismatch between your .NET 7 application and the legacy .NET Framework assemblies that WPF projects often reference by default. When your code attempts to load the WPF window on Windows, the runtime is looking for specific version 4.0.0.0 assemblies from the older .NET Framework, which are not present in the modern .NET 7 runtime environment. This happens because the WPF project was likely configured to target the classic .NET Framework, causing the build output to depend on system files that do not exist in a cross-platform .NET 7 context.

To resolve this without reverting your main application to .NET Framework, you must reconfigure your WPF project to target .NET 7 or .NET 8 explicitly instead of .NET Framework. By updating the TargetFramework property in your WPF project file to a modern version like net7.0-windows, the build system will generate assemblies that are compatible with your main application's runtime. This ensures that the PresentationFramework assembly is loaded from the correct location within the .NET 7 SDK rather than the system-wide .NET Framework directories.

cousin projects in the .NET ecosystem often face similar binding issues when mixing different runtime targets, but the solution lies in ensuring all components speak the same language. Once you align the WPF project to the same .NET version as your main application, you should also verify that the project file includes the necessary Windows-specific SDK references to guarantee that WPF controls are correctly bundled. This alignment prevents the runtime from attempting to resolve legacy dependencies that are simply not available in your cross-platform deployment model.

After updating the target framework, you will likely need to clean your solution and rebuild it from scratch to remove any cached references to the old .NET Framework assemblies. It is also crucial to ensure that your build configuration is set to publish or run in a mode that supports Windows desktop applications, such as using the WindowsDesktop target framework condition. This step guarantees that the necessary WPF binaries are included in your output directory and are correctly resolved when the application launches on a Windows machine.

Finally, testing your application on both Windows and macOS will confirm that the dynamic linking works as intended, with the WPF window appearing on Windows and the Cocoa window on Mac. By standardizing on a modern .NET version for all UI components, you maintain the cross-platform nature of your application while avoiding the pitfalls of legacy assembly loading. This approach allows you to keep your main project in .NET 7
